sentences.text: I was able to fight, if not for, yes, a common enemy, to-- I was again, a human being, not a half-mensch-- if I could call it.
Because I was not a simple toy in the hands of the Germans, which could do with me when they wanted to kill me, and how they wanted to do, or force me to labor.
It was not easy, but I-- I could fight, and I could eat, and I could-- could live again.
It helped me a lot.
Because it-- it made me again thinking as a human being.
As a-- in the camp I tried not to lose my-- my will to survive, you see.
And to help others to survive.
I lied, sometimes, in order to give them hope.
And they all waited for me.
I was in Israel, and some recognized me and said, do you know what you helped us with this?
Did that-- any of that feeling rub off on your family?
The fact that you'd made such a contribution towards fighting the Germans?
My prison family?
No, your-- your parents at the time, for the time that they stayed alive.
And your sister, and your other relations.
No, they didn't know about me.
If I am alive or not.
No, but when they found you, afterwards, when you were together.
And they'd known that you fought against the Germans.
Then, as they saw me going in the army, to the front, there I could tell, right.
You see, I wasn't permitted, military security, to tell where I am or what I am doing, you see.
But they realized.
I sent them photos.
Here I was a soldier.
Here I was a radio sergeant major.
And I could send them money.
And then in Germany, we had trophies we got instead of money.
We could get from the trophy, certain thing for a certain sum.
So I could make a purse and send them back, which helped them.
They were German, from French where they had stores.
You see, so I could help them.
Yes.
In Romania.
There were two kinds of people.
There were people who came out from concentration camp, from deportation, concentration camp, others which were in prison for-- because they were Jews, and the different [INAUDIBLE].
[INAUDIBLE] from forced labor, or something.
And they didn't have a home where to go.
They didn't have families, mostly.
So they were glad that they had somebody to bring them.
Their morale was down, you could image.
And you had-- some were murders.
Some were used to steal from the concentration camp.
That is what's in your-- to organize, was the [INAUDIBLE].
And here we had-- as a group, held they're idealists, which came from school, driven by the Zionist ideal to build up a country of their own, not to suffer anymore in the [NON-ENGLISH].
One of this group was my present wife, [INAUDIBLE].
The camp was organized with two camps, in that one was in town working in a brick factory, which was conducted by a former higher SS officer.
It was [INAUDIBLE], the families was a father and son, yes.
But he fought in the German army.
Yes.
And I knew about it, and a bit of pressure, and with this I got out what I wanted.
And they worked there.
They got paid.
And I could train them with work because of that [? week. ?] I made everything-- we got from Joint, also help.
I tried the cultural level.
I introduced fried [? ham, ?] evening kiddush.
I did not-- they did not go-- they did not have any interest, [INAUDIBLE] well, one or two which were religious, you see.
We had three married coupled, survivor of Auschwitz, one [INAUDIBLE], each married.
And they were glad I organized them.
I was perhaps a bit straight-- too straight.
There was-- use military, and they had to be clean, and to clean themselves, and to work, doesn't do any harm as long as you get proper food, which I tried to give them.
I organized-- at the time it was 100 gram bread per day.
Because Romania was also a very bad situation.
I gave them 400 to breakfast.
As you would ask how I took from the bakery.
I made the bakery.
I trained boys as baker.
Some knew a bit, and some baker.
One of the-- one of the married girls, which was our-- was cooking, the other helped.
Right?
We made this state in state.
I trained them.
I read Hebrew to teach them.
And military training [INAUDIBLE], Judo.
Then they divorced in September.
My friend, [NON-ENGLISH] brought a transport to Yugoslavia and over this-- one of these ships.
Legal aliyah to Palestine.
It was also-- sent to [NON-ENGLISH] and sent to Cyprus in the camp, by the English already.
And I started training.
We got, from Palestine, they sent us special from Parma, training officer.
Special [NON-ENGLISH] to train military training-- to give serious military training.
So in our agricultural part, which was on an estate, was joint.
[INAUDIBLE] took over.
We trained in the forest.
We trained them and we had old poor military training.
We had, every four weeks, a group of 500, 600.
From different parts which group-- training groups.
There the Jewish was trained, militarily trained.
And they got so strong in Judo and this Israeli force.
And what is expected?
The discipline they lacked, which was important.
Were you involved with the military training as well as running the other camp?
I was-- officially had to keep the organization running.
The logistics, the liaison with the local police and the [INAUDIBLE] police, [INAUDIBLE] them up, you see.
We-- the two camps were trained to go to Palestine as soon as we get.
In the meantime, in Burgas two ships arrived, [NON-ENGLISH]--
All the time they changed.
All the time, groups.
They had a few thousand which went through.
Romanian, Israel, also parts from Hungary.
Jews, all were Jews.
From Hungary, we had from Russia, which escaped from the army.
And deserters, but which we joined, they knew already, militarily.
No.
No, all forged documents, and some were stationed with units in Romania, and they escaped, and they wanted a way.
Most lost their families [? in ?] here.
Not.
You see, it's, again, I was, again, the Russian captain.
I could confiscate flour, make bakery.
I'd go and give them IOUs in the villages, which perhaps today they wait, still, to be paid from Stalin, from the Russians, there, which were worthless.
But I used them to make-- I bought whole carriages of watermelons.
Up to the aliyah.
It was the 24th of December, all right, we tried--
[INAUDIBLE] 46.
And I organized these groups.
We had, already, connection with the Jewish agency, and to [NON-ENGLISH] and with other group from over the border in Hungary, into Slovakia, which were armed.
And we prepared to go in to fight.
In the meantime, we had also to look after the Jewish population.
Liaison with the Jewish population and to organize their aliyah.

Because I brought, over the border, people from Hungary, which they couldn't speak a word Romanian, but I was there from representative of the International Red Cross, too, had official papers to bring them.
I brought them over the border as this-- this money, and this, and help-- joint help with money.
We left Romania on 24th of December, 1946.
We had-- I had to organize trains.
Romania was divided in eight groups.
And from-- we had to bring-- it was the-- the Jewish [NON-ENGLISH], or answer to the exodus.
As they send the people back to Germany.
You know this.
And we had two ships, [NON-ENGLISH].
They were banana boats.
We had to prepare them.
And they arrived in Burgas where they were prepared for the aliyah.
This ship would have been only for 2,000, 2,200 soldiers in wartime, and we put on these two ships 17,000 people, from Bulgaria and from Romania, mostly.
And we had to organize the train.
My part was southern Transylvania, that is on this side of the Carpathian.
Organize the train.
Organize one-way passports from the Romanian authority.
It was all bought.
And we went up there.
Women and children, I had about 1,700 people on my train.
In every train carriage I had one of my boys, who was trained, and which listen to me, they got on [INAUDIBLE].
There were women and children.
There were problems, they were not luxury trains.
It was very good [INAUDIBLE] again.
But we went over.
Went first, because they under the pressure of the British authorities there went-- didn't let the ship stay, so they went over to Bulgaria, Burgas.
But they had to leave on the 20-- on the 26th, if I [INAUDIBLE]. 26th or 27th. 26th, we had to leave Burgas, and being a Saturday.
We had to leave at 12 o'clock.
To leave the [INAUDIBLE] waters of Burgas.
We had to bring-- mine was the last transfer to arrive.
Had some incidents with the train because some religious didn't want to go on Saturday.
And they paid, [NON-ENGLISH], the station commander where we stopped.
And we went on and on.
We arrived in time in Burgas.
Went on the boat.
There, again, I was in leadership.
These were Italian, Spanish, sailors, under Jewish command, former American Navy officers.
And Israeli, because Israel was already on the way.
We came to the Dardanelles, again, under pressure of the British we were stopped.
This is our [INAUDIBLE], and so on.
But, again, their Jewish representative, which paid, again, [INAUDIBLE] of our holy days [? at ?] people, and to pass at Dardanelle.
As we came out from the Aegean Sea, we were surrounded, first caught by the fleet.
First, two planes appeared [INAUDIBLE], and then 13 warships surrounded us.
Two submarines surrounded us and asked us to give in.
We said we [INAUDIBLE] the ships-- let the ships sink.
We agreed not to go to Tobruk as they wanted, the British fleet.
It was open sea, without permit.
We were under Panamanian flag, and we were caught and surrounded, put it blue white flag up.
And we were brought on the 1st of January to Cyprus.
Near Famagusta in Caraolos, the so-called summer camp, which already had five camps.
We brought-- part went to Caraolos, part went to the winter camp in Dekhelia.
There I started, again, working, organizing the groups and this other small military part.
And this liaison with the British HQ, now.
Speaking English, so I could deal with them.
And supplies and food which came in.
And then I was, about eight months, camp leader.
In charge of the camp because we changed from all the parties.
And I was in charge of 61 camp.
Till my aliyah.
That was in 1949, in February, '49 when I came to Israel, already.
There, to the front.
In Cyprus?
Yes, I married.
[LAUGHS] I married in 22nd of April, 1946, I married.
Seven, I married.
Yes.
Yes, she was on my ship.
She was my aide.
Second in command.
In April. 22nd of April, '47.
In '48 I arrived in-- in Israel. '49, sorry.
In Israel.
I closed the camp.
I was the last.
There's a deficit of 3,000 people from whom I got food.
And I asked by the commander, last day, where are the 3,000 people?
So I answered, you have watched that.
There were city roads of barbed wire and observation towers with armed soldiers, with 1,000 tanks around.
So I asked them where they are.
We helped them to escape.
In Israel we came straight for Haifa.
We came to Shavei Zion.
That is a moshav near Nahariyyah.
And there we worked and fought.
You see, we had well organized all ready, they were [INAUDIBLE].
[INAUDIBLE] to fight and work in the moshav.
And then I was in-- went over to Nahariyyah, the nearest place.
And the army called me up.
I was up in the army.
And there I was needed.
I had the training already, military.
My group was soldier already.
These were recruited, most did not know.
And to need it for the front.
It was war.
Oh, you see, it was-- you got free, so you went back to Nahariyyah and worked.
I worked in different jobs.
But because the school started to reorganize schools, so I worked as a teacher.
And when I was not in the army I was a local mobilization officer.
Obviously I get-- got the job.
You see, there, it didn't have telephone at the time.
It was one telephone and-- or two telephones the whole Nahariyyah, which was a village.
Now it's a nice town.
But by [INAUDIBLE], they came with the motorbike, a soldier brought me the mobilization order.
So I had a few men with soldiers in the reserve, which I announced in front of the [INAUDIBLE].
They went out and called the other group.
And so it went down.
In a certain hour you had to move [INAUDIBLE] [NON-ENGLISH].
That was our uniform.
She was working in Nahariyyah.
They had a home for wounded soldiers.
And she worked in the hotels there.
They had majordomos, [INAUDIBLE].
Till my wife fell ill and I had little scolding incident, you could call it so.
I was sent also for the army.
I was a reconnaissance officer.
I was sent over to the territory over Syrian territory.
Came back.
My job-- I worked in the bank the time-- was gone.
All right, during the day I worked in the bank, in the evening I worked in the school, the grammar school, the evening grammar school.
Teaching children which could not have studied normally in schools and wanted to learn.
So I taught them.
And I came back, my job was taken by one-- a religious one who did not go to the army.
A Dutch man, nice chap, but the-- the chairman told me-- boss, manager of the bank told me, we don't need people who promenade in the army all the time.
I was called up, you know?
It was something-- we mobilized.
And so I-- '55 I came to England on a work permit.
And I worked here.
First in-- in the industry, as a specialist in the food industry.
In [INAUDIBLE] City.
Afterwards they found of my knowledge of languages, my technical knowledge.
And I worked, officially it was scientific information consultant, which was a part of intelligence work.
Scientific intelligence work.
Mostly Russian products, Chinese, and so on.
I came on a work permit.
My wife was British by birth, through her father.
But until Prince Philip, also he was soldier in the British army-- navy.
He was still not British citizen.
Also, he married the queen.
Then come [INAUDIBLE], that facilitated.
But I had already had my work permit to work in Britain, so I came as food specialist-- food technician.
I did, but I did not really study.
It was special course, which I made scientific intelligence officer from the home office.
And I applied for the British citizenship.
My children were born.
My son-- two sons were born.
And to lived happily further here.
And one-- no, '56, sorry. '56, 12th of June.
The other, 3rd of February, 1956.
Not married, but they studied, both, and are in the jobs which help the community.
Not only the Jewish community, the general's [? estate. ?]
The older son, Selwyn, is a senior executive at the [?
Center ?] to the University of London.
And the younger son is doctor trained, and he's-- he's a senior lecturer and director of the Bloomfield Institute of Intensive Care Medicine.
And registrar-- senior registrar at, oh, University College, you see.
But he's lecturing all over the world.
Yes, she worked until she had the children.
She worked in-- first in tailoring, what she could work, you see.
A bit, you know, not much.
You see, her father was born here.
Her grandfather lived here, so she was British born abroad.
And that she was also a survivor, being under the German occupation in Romania.
But under the care of the Swiss, which was representing the British interest in occupied Romania, so she survived.
Immediately.
I was 40 years, it took me, only.
But, you see, I made a-- nobody asked me if I am British or not.
And so I worked in intelligence in the past.
Vetted- was vetted, both by the English and the American.
And the they took this as a matter of fact that I am British citizen, you see, which I was not.
And when it comes as 40 years, I applied for.
In the mean time, I was also-- I passed the course at the home office for British intelligence officer.
I got the rank of, at the time, a senior intelligence-- scientific intelligence officer, that was attached to the civil defense.
And it was the cold war.
And so I worked training, also, teaching and training and-- so by day I was scientific intelligence, as my bread and butter.
And the other time I worked for the home office in civil defense, police training in nuclear science, which was the first [? date. ?]
Oh, relative, I could make a living without my wife working outside, and bringing up my children.
I didn't-- from work, would never become a millionaire.
I didn't become it.
I didn't try.
But I was a free man, you see, respected and all.
I worked, also, in the community.
I lived in Palmer's Green.
First in Tottenham, then in Palmer's Green.
I was active building bridges with the non-Jewish community, because you have to-- I-- I got friendly with the locals, the British.
I was chairman in the local Church of England school.
I was a Parents Teacher Association.
I was in a police committee.
I was in the local residents' association.
All where I could help build bridges with, to show that a Jew is not only here to exploit.
Intellect is [INAUDIBLE] recognized and they liked me.
Yes and not.
Not direct.
It didn't-- if you could call antisemitism, not proper antisemitism.
Not knowing-- many didn't know that I'm a Jew.
I had, for instance, last Rosh Hashanah I went to shul, synagogue, and I was dressed up.
A colleague of mine, which was for years with me on the various committees, English-English, told me, oh, you are going-- it was Sunday morning-- oh you are going to church so early?
I said, no, I'm going to synagogue.
We have the new year.
What, are you Jewish?
She just could not understand.
I'll give you an example.
I was in uniform, in civil defense, and I was asked to a party.
If I ask for a dietary food, Jewish dietary, I said, yes.
And when I sat here it was a major general next to me, and the other was the Bishop of Sussex.
And they were surprised seeing me eating different things than them.
Saying, oh, are you Jewish?
I said, yes.
Oh, it's very nice that you keep to the customs of the faith of your religion.
That was it.
We've come to the end of this tape, now.
